input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers real‑time monitoring of all sensor streams, predictive simulation of energy and climate performance, lifecycle‑wide optimisation of HVAC and lighting set‑points, closed‑loop control commands to actuators, and an interactive 3D visualisation dashboard. Stakeholders (facility managers, engineers, occupants) can query live status, run what‑if scenarios, receive anomaly alerts, and inspect an immersive Unreal Engine representation of the building for decision support.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system employs a model‑driven framework that defines a modular architecture with explicit extension points. Core components include a BIM‑based static model, a real‑time data ingestion layer built on Eclipse Hono, a digital‑twin state management service provided by Eclipse Ditto, and a 3‑D visualization front‑end realised with Unreal Engine. Orchestration is achieved by coupling Hono’s MQTT/HTTP gateways to Ditto’s twin management APIs, while the framework automatically generates runtime interfaces from domain semantics. Resource management is handled through the modular plug‑in mechanism, allowing independent deployment of each component and coordinated lifecycle via a central configuration service.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is realised through the Eclipse Hono connector, which supports MQTT, HTTP and CoAP for IoT device ingestion, and through Eclipse Ditto, which exposes a REST/WebSocket API adhering to the Ditto Twin Model for interoperability. The framework adopts standard integration patterns (e.g., publish‑subscribe via MQTT, twin synchronization via Ditto’s twin‑update streams) to interoperate with enterprise ERP, BIM repositories, and other digital twins. Unreal Engine consumes the twin state via Ditto’s WebSocket interface, enabling real‑time visualization. This architecture enables loose coupling and protocol‑agnostic data exchange across heterogeneous platforms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The framework enriches BIM models with three layers of computational artifacts: (1) geometric models inherited from the BIM representation of building spaces and elements; (2) behavioral models that encode sensor, actuator, and controller logic defined by Building Control Engineers directly within the BIM environment; and (3) analytical models that are introduced by DT Engineers as custom services or analytics plugins. Data sources comprise real‑time streams originating from the physical sensors and actuators, which are continuously synchronized with the digital side via the framework’s real‑time data exchange mechanism. Model management follows Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) principles: enriched BIM models are transformed into structured intermediate representations and automatically compiled into deployable Digital Twin artifacts, establishing explicit relationships between geometric entities and their associated behavioral components. These models constitute the semantic foundation for downstream visualization, control, and analytics services.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical actuators are represented as explicit device elements within the enriched BIM model. Building Control Engineers model actuators (e.g., HVAC, lighting, shading devices) together with their control logic; the Digital Twin exposes these actuators to Facility Managers through interactive visualization and remote control interfaces. Actuation commands are transmitted via the framework’s real‑time data exchange layer, enabling bidirectional control of the physical infrastructure. The document does not specify actuator types, ranges, safety constraints, or addressing schemes beyond their inclusion in the BIM‑based semantic model.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are similarly introduced into the BIM model by Building Control Engineers, creating a unified semantic description of measurement points (e.g., temperature, humidity, occupancy). The framework continuously streams sensor readings to the Digital Twin using its real‑time data exchange capability, allowing Facility Managers to monitor building states. The documentation does not provide concrete sensor categories, sampling frequencies, transmission rates, precision, or spatial distribution details; it only asserts that sensor data are integrated into the DT for synchronization and visualization.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is addressed by extending static BIM geometry with behavioral definitions for sensors, actuators, and control logic, thereby reducing the semantic gap between the physical building and its digital replica. Validation is implied through the MDE workflow that automatically extracts structured representations from the enriched BIM and generates deployable DT artifacts, minimizing manual errors. Continuous real‑time data exchange serves as an implicit verification mechanism, ensuring that the digital state reflects the physical reality. No explicit procedures for uncertainty quantification, error correction, or formal quality‑assurance processes are described in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The DT Framework provides real-time data exchange that continuously synchronises the physical building with its digital counterpart. Sensor and other live operational data generated in the building are streamed into the digital twin through the framework’s standardized extension points; runtime configuration management allows the data flow to be adjusted without redeploying the whole system.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Through the same bidirectional data exchange, the DT Framework forwards control actions issued by Facility Managers or automated control logic to the physical building. Commands are routed via the framework’s central hub to the addressed devices, enabling real-time monitoring and control of building systems.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The solution adopts a modular architecture with standardized extension points that support interoperability among heterogeneous building systems. Real-time data exchange is achieved over the network infrastructure managed by the DT Framework, whose non‑functional requirements include scalability, maintainability and accessibility; the framework can be reconfigured at runtime to adapt to changing network conditions.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The framework defines a four‑stage life‑cycle: (1) Device Modeling – Building Control Engineers enrich BIM with sensor, actuator and controller entities; (2) Model Extraction – Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) transforms the enriched BIM into a structured meta‑model; (3) Artifact Generation & Deployment – the meta‑model is automatically compiled into deployable DT services and runtime containers; (4) Operation & Maintenance – real‑time data exchange synchronises the physical building with the deployed twin, while facility managers monitor and control via interactive visualisation. Transitions are managed by automated model‑to‑code pipelines and governed by role‑based responsibilities (engineer, manager, DT engineer) as defined in Table I.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Twinning follows a model‑driven engineering process. The enriched BIM model is the single source of truth; automated model‑to‑code transformations generate the DT artefacts, removing manual coding. Consistency checks between the BIM semantics and the generated services constitute the primary quality‑assurance activity. Runtime verification is performed by observing the real‑time data exchange to confirm that physical‑digital state synchronisation is correct. When the physical system or operational requirements change, the Building Control Engineer updates the BIM enrichment and the framework re‑executes the transformation pipeline, regenerating only the impacted artefacts; full redeployment is not required. Extension of functionality is achieved by plugging custom services into the modular architecture through the predefined extension points, allowing evolutionary growth of the twin without disrupting existing services.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The twin provides real‑time monitoring of system states through continuous data exchange and presents the information via immersive interactive visualisation. Facility Managers use these visualisations to observe sensor readings, actuator status and controller outputs, and to issue control commands directly to the physical infrastructure. Decision support is therefore based on up‑to‑date operational data and the ability to simulate actions through the visual interface; the framework does not specify advanced analytics, prediction models, or confidence metrics.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The solution is built on Building Information Modeling (BIM) as the core standards backbone and exposes standardized extension points defined by the framework for plug‑in integration. It targets compatibility with existing AECO‑specific systems and standards that rely on BIM representations. No additional industry standards (e.g., IFC, ISO 19443) are explicitly mentioned in the document.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

The abstract explicitly mentions 'We demonstrate the practical application of our approach in a Living Lab case study' and refers to the 'architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO) domain'. The introduction also details 'infrastructure DTs' and specific projects like 'TwinLight' and 'BOREALIS' involving building automation and lighting control. Therefore, the claim that this information is not in the document is false.

The extraction accurately reflects the services described in the abstract and introduction, such as 'real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization' and using 'Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization'. It correctly identifies the capability for 'bidirectional communication' (interpreted as closed-loop control) and the role of the 'Facility Manager' who 'Monitors system states'. However, it includes minor speculative details like 'anomaly alerts' and 'what-if scenarios' which are not explicitly found in the provided text.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', which is incorrect. The source documents list several specific tools. The abstract states: 'The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange and leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization.' The introduction also references 'Eclipse Hono [15] and Ditto [16]' and 'Unreal Engine 5 [17]'. Building Information Modeling (BIM) is also highlighted as a core enabler. Because the text contains explicit details about these technologies, the claim that they are missing is invalid.

The extracted description is accurate and well-supported by the source text. It also accurately describes the orchestration and modular architecture. Quotes supporting this include: 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization', and 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms'.

The extracted description is accurate and grounded in the source text. block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

The description correctly identifies that actuators are modeled within the BIM environment by Building Control Engineers and exposed to Facility Managers. It accurately mentions the use of the real-time data exchange layer for commands. The extraction correctly notes the absence of specific technical details like ranges or safety constraints in the provided text, aligning with the general descriptions found in the source.

The extraction correctly states that sensors are modeled in BIM to create semantic descriptions and that data is streamed via real-time exchange. It accurately reflects the source's high-level overview while correctly noting the absence of specific technical specifications (e.g., sampling frequencies, precision) in the documents.

The extraction correctly infers that fidelity is improved by adding behavioral definitions to static geometry, addressing the 'semantic gap' mentioned in the text. It accurately identifies the MDE workflow and real-time synchronization as the primary validation mechanisms. The score reflects the accurate assessment of the provided documents, which indeed lack explicit formal QA or uncertainty quantification procedures.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
